---
title: "One-Way ANOVA Activity Worksheet"
subtitle: "STAT 252 — Spring 2026 · Cal Poly SLO"
format:
  docx:
    number-sections: false
    toc: false
    highlight-style: github
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{=openxml}
<w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Normal"/></w:pPr>
<w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t></w:r></w:p>
```

---

## Student Information

| Field | Response |
|:------|:---------|
| **Name** | ________________________________ |
| **Student ID** | ________________________________ |
| **Section / Time** | ________________________________ |
| **Participant Code** | ________________________________ |
| **Date** | ________________________________ |

---

\newpage

## Part 1 — Context and Research Question

### Background

Researchers in education often want to know whether different instructional or study methods lead to different learning outcomes. To test this rigorously, we need a statistical method that can compare the *means* of three or more groups simultaneously while accounting for natural variation among individuals.

**One-Way ANOVA** (Analysis of Variance) is the appropriate method when:

- The **explanatory variable** is categorical with two or more levels (groups)
- The **response variable** is quantitative (numeric)
- Observations are independent across and within groups
- Within-group variances are roughly equal across groups

### Research Question

A researcher wants to know whether **mean exam scores** differ significantly across **three different teaching methods** (Method A, Method B, and Method C). After randomly assigning 75 students (25 per group) to one of the three methods, all students completed the same standardized exam (scored 0–100).

**1.** State the research question in your own words:

> *Does the _________________________ differ significantly across the three _________________________?*

---

## Part 2 — Identifying Variables

**2.** Identify the **explanatory variable**:

| | |
|:---|:---|
| **Name of variable** | ________________________________ |
| **Type** | ☐ Categorical  ☐ Quantitative |
| **Number of levels (groups)** | ________________________________ |
| **Names of the levels** | ________________________________ |

**3.** Identify the **response variable**:

| | |
|:---|:---|
| **Name of variable** | ________________________________ |
| **Type** | ☐ Categorical  ☐ Quantitative |
| **Measurement scale / units** | ________________________________ |

**4.** In this study, which variable is being *explained* and which variable does the *explaining*?

> The **response** variable is _________________________ because it is the outcome we are trying to explain.
>
> The **explanatory** variable is _________________________ because it defines the groups we are comparing.

---

## Part 3 — Conceptual Understanding

### Within-Group Variability (The Noise)

Within-group variability measures how spread out individual scores are **around their own group mean (ȳᵢ)**. It exists even when all members of a group received the exact same treatment — it is simply natural variation among individuals.

**5.** Define within-group variability in your own words:

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

**6.** Fill in the blanks:

> Large within-group variability means that individuals within the same group are very **_________________________** from each other.
>
> In ANOVA, within-group variability is called the **_________________________** (noise / signal) because it makes it **_________________________** (harder / easier) to detect real group differences.
>
> Within-group variability is the **_________________________** (numerator / denominator) of the F-ratio.

**7.** In the dot plot below *(sketch or describe what you see on the slides)*, the scores within each group are:

> ☐ Tightly clustered  ☐ Widely spread

This represents **_________________________** within-group variability.

---

### Between-Group Variability (The Signal)

Between-group variability measures how far apart the **group means (ȳᵢ)** are from the **grand mean (ȳ̄)** — the overall average across all observations. This is the *signal* that ANOVA is designed to detect.

**8.** Define between-group variability in your own words:

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

**9.** Fill in the blanks:

> Large between-group variability means the group means (ȳᵢ) are far from the **_________________________** (ȳ̄).
>
> In ANOVA, between-group variability is the **_________________________** (noise / signal) and is the **_________________________** (numerator / denominator) of the F-ratio.

**10.** Suppose the three group means are ȳ_A = 72, ȳ_B = 85, ȳ_C = 91, and the grand mean is ȳ̄ = 82.7.

> Which group mean is farthest from the grand mean? **_________________________**
>
> Does this suggest large or small between-group variability? **_________________________**

---

## Part 4 — The F-Ratio and Test Statistic

The **F-statistic** (test statistic for One-Way ANOVA) is the ratio of between-group variability to within-group variability:

$$F = \frac{\text{Between-Group Variability (Signal)}}{\text{Within-Group Variability (Noise)}} = \frac{MS_{Between}}{MS_{Within}}$$

**11.** Complete the formula using words:

> $$F = \frac{\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_}{\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_}$$

**12.** Interpret the following F values:

| F value | What it tells us about the data |
|:--------|:--------------------------------|
| F = 12.4 | ________________________________ |
| F ≈ 1.0  | ________________________________ |
| F = −2.1 | ________________________________ *(Note: Is this possible? Why or why not?)* |

**13.** Fill in the blanks:

> A **large** F-statistic means between-group differences are **_________________________** relative to within-group noise — this is **_________________________** (strong / weak) evidence that group means differ.
>
> A **small** F-statistic (≈ 1) means group differences are about the same size as **_________________________** — little evidence the groups truly differ.

**14.** The F-statistic is always ≥ 0. Explain why in one sentence:

> _____________________________________________________________________________

---

## Part 5 — The p-value

The **p-value** tells us how likely it is to observe an F-statistic as large as (or larger than) the one we calculated, **assuming the null hypothesis is true** (i.e., all group means are equal).

$$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C \quad \text{(no difference in group means)}$$
$$H_a: \text{At least one group mean is different}$$

**15.** Fill in the blanks:

> A **small p-value** (e.g., p = 0.003) means it is very **_________________________** (likely / unlikely) to observe this data if H₀ were true.
>
> This provides **_________________________** (strong / weak) evidence **_________________________** (for / against) H₀, supporting a causal claim that teaching method **_________________________** student exam scores.
>
> A **large p-value** (e.g., p = 0.42) means the observed differences could easily be explained by **_________________________** alone.

**16.** Causal language check — replace "prove" with appropriate language:

> ❌ *"An F = 8.47 with p = 0.003 proves that teaching method causes differences in exam scores."*
>
> ✅ *"An F = 8.47 with p = 0.003 _________________________ a causal claim that teaching method _________________________ exam scores."*

*(Use phrases like "provides evidence for," "supports," or "gives us reason to believe")*

---

## Part 6 — Interpreting Results

**Suppose the ANOVA output is:**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F | p-value |
|:-------|:---|:---|:---|:---|:--------|
| Between Groups | 2 | 1,234 | 617 | 8.47 | 0.003 |
| Within Groups | 72 | 5,246 | 72.9 | | |
| **Total** | **74** | **6,480** | | | |

**17.** What is the value of the test statistic?  **F = _______**

**18.** Interpret the test statistic in context:

> The F-statistic of _______ means the between-group variability is approximately _______ times larger than the within-group variability. This is **_________________________** (consistent / inconsistent) with there being real differences among teaching methods.

**19.** What is the p-value?  **p = _______**

**20.** Interpret the p-value in context:

> If all three teaching methods produced identical mean exam scores (H₀ true), there would be only a _______ % chance of observing an F-statistic this large or larger by random chance alone.

**21.** Make a statistical decision (circle or fill in):

> Since p = 0.003 is **_________________________** (less than / greater than) α = 0.05,
>
> we **_________________________** (reject / fail to reject) H₀.

**22.** Write a conclusion in context using causally appropriate language:

> We have **_________________________** evidence to _________________________ the claim that teaching method affects mean exam scores. Specifically, the data _________________________ that at least one teaching method produces a different mean exam score than the others (F = 8.47, p = 0.003).

---

## Part 7 — Connecting to ANOVA Concepts

**23.** The F-statistic **increases** when (check all that apply):

> ☐ Between-group variability increases (group means move farther apart)
> ☐ Within-group variability decreases (individuals become more similar within groups)
> ☐ Sample size increases
> ☐ All of the above

**24.** The p-value **decreases** (becomes more significant) when:

> ☐ The F-statistic is larger
> ☐ Sample sizes are larger
> ☐ Both of the above

**25.** The equal variance condition (homoscedasticity) requires:

> The **_________________________** (spread / center) of scores within each group should be **_________________________** across all groups.
>
> A rough check: the largest group standard deviation should be no more than approximately **_______×** the smallest.

**26.** In one sentence, explain what "statistical evidence" means in the context of ANOVA:

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

---

## Part 8 — AI Interaction Log *(For AI Treatment Groups Only)*

*Control group students: skip to Part 9.*

### Your Prompts and Key Takeaways

**Prompt 1** *(paste or summarize your first AI prompt)*:

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

**Key concept you learned or clarified from the response:**

> _____________________________________________________________________________

**Prompt 2** *(paste or summarize)*:

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

**Key concept you learned or clarified:**

> _____________________________________________________________________________

**Prompt 3** *(if applicable)*:

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

**Key concept you learned or clarified:**

> _____________________________________________________________________________

---

## Part 9 — Reflection

**27.** Which concept was most challenging for you today? Why?

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

**28.** In your own words, explain how between-group variability and within-group variability work **together** in the F-ratio to help us make decisions:

> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________
>
> _____________________________________________________________________________

**29.** Without looking at your notes, complete this sentence:

> *One-Way ANOVA tests whether the _________________________ of _________________________ or more groups are significantly different, by comparing _________________________ variability (signal) to _________________________ variability (noise) using the _________________________ statistic.*

---

*Submit this worksheet to your instructor at the end of class along with your Canvas Post-Test.*
